text: Developing high-performance, stable, and cost-effective electrodes for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is essential for advancing clean energy technologies such as water splitting and rechargeable metal-air batteries. Here, we report the synthesis of hybrid porous nanowire arrays consisting of strongly interacting $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4$ and carbon, fabricated via the direct carbonization of a cobalt-based metal-organic framework (MOF) grown on copper foil. The resulting $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4\text{C-NA}$ material features a high surface area ($251\text{ m}^2\text{ g}^{-1}$) and significant carbon content ($52.1\text{ wt}\%$), allowing it to serve as a binder-free, integrated working electrode. In alkaline media, this electrode exhibits exceptional OER activity with a low onset potential of $1.47\text{ V}$ vs. RHE and achieves a current density of $10.0\text{ mA cm}^{-2}$ at merely $1.52\text{ V}$. Furthermore, it demonstrates a high Faradaic efficiency of $99.3\%$ and remarkable durability, maintaining stable performance for over 30 hours. The superior catalytic behavior, which outperforms many state-of-the-art noble metal and transition metal catalysts, is attributed to the synergistic effects between the in situ formed carbon and $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4$, as well as the unique nanowire architecture that enhances charge/mass transport and gas bubble release. Additionally, the material functions as an efficient bi-functional catalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) via a four-electron pathway, highlighting its potential for reversible oxygen electrochemistry in next-generation regenerative energy devices.
